senses.definition: Feeling or showing very strong love and admiration for someone or something.
examples: She smiled at her child with adoring eyes.
The singer waved to his adoring fans.
He is an adoring husband who supports her career.

senses.definition: In a way that shows very strong love, admiration, or devotion.
examples: She gazed adoringly at her newborn baby.
He speaks adoringly of his wife whenever he has the chance.
The dog looked up at its owner adoringly as she prepared its food.

senses.definition: To make something or someone more beautiful by adding decorations.
examples: Flowers adorned the entrance to the hall.
She adorned her hair with small white ribbons.
Colorful paintings adorn the walls of the restaurant.

senses.definition: To add something that gives a person or thing more honor or distinction.
examples: Her honesty and kindness adorn her character.
Several awards adorn his long career.
Historic statues adorn the city, adding to its prestige.

senses.definition: The act of decorating or the state of being decorated, especially in an elaborate way.
examples: The ornate adornation of the palace impressed all the visitors.
Too much adornation can make a design look crowded.
Religious festivals often involve the adornation of statues and altars.

senses.definition: Decorated in a way that makes something more beautiful or attractive.
examples: The walls were adorned with family photographs.
She wore a dress adorned with tiny pearls.
The cake was heavily adorned with fresh fruit.

senses.definition: A person or thing that decorates or beautifies something.
examples: The interior designer acted as the chief adorner of the hotel lobby.
In the story, the goddess is described as the adorner of the earth.
Jewelry can be a powerful adorner of even the simplest outfit.

senses.definition: People or things that decorate or beautify something.
examples: The temple's adorners worked day and night before the festival.
Artists and architects were the main adorners of the ancient city.
Flowers and candles are common adorners of wedding venues.

senses.definition: Present participle of adorn: decorating something to make it more beautiful.
examples: Garlands were adorning the streets during the celebration.
Paintings adorning the corridor give the office a warmer atmosphere.
Lights adorning the trees created a magical scene.

senses.definition: Serving to decorate or beautify something.
examples: Adorning details on the ceiling caught everyone’s attention.
She chose adorning accessories to match her dress.
The adorning carvings on the door are handmade.

senses.definition: In a way that decorates or adds beauty.
examples: Flowers hung adorningly from the balcony railings.
Lights were strung adorningly around the garden.
Jewels sparkled adorningly on her wrists and neck.

senses.definition: Something added to make a person or thing look more beautiful.
examples: She wore only a simple necklace as an adornment.
The carvings serve as both support and adornment for the building.
Seasonal adornment of the shop windows attracts many customers.

senses.definition: Decorative things used to make someone or something more attractive.
examples: The room was filled with colorful adornments.
She removed the heavy adornments from her hair at the end of the day.
Holiday adornments hung from every window of the house.

senses.definition: Theodor W. Adorno, a German philosopher, sociologist, and musicologist known for his work in critical theory.
examples: Adorno wrote extensively about culture and mass media.
Many students read Adorno in their critical theory courses.
Adorno's ideas about the culture industry remain influential.

senses.definition: Decorative elements or ornaments, especially in some varieties of English influenced by Spanish.
examples: The dancers wore traditional adornos on their costumes.
Architectural adornos gave the building a distinctive style.
She added a few adornos to the design to make it less plain.

senses.definition: Third person singular of adorn: decorates something or makes it more beautiful.
examples: A large painting adorns the far wall of the room.
Snow adorns the tops of the mountains in winter.
A gold label adorns each bottle.

senses.definition: Placed back to back, especially in heraldry.
examples: The shield shows two lions adorsed on a blue field.
The design features birds adorsed along the central line.
In heraldic art, animals may be drawn adorsed to indicate separation.

senses.definition: Many small difficulties, worries, or busy activities, often seen as unnecessary fuss.
examples: She managed the wedding arrangements without too many ados.
There were endless ados before the project finally started.
He dislikes the social ados that come with big events.

senses.definition: Impregnation by external contact without sexual intercourse, especially in biology.
examples: Adosculation can be observed in some species of fish.
The textbook briefly mentioned adosculation as a reproductive method.
Historically, the term adosculation was used in both zoology and botany.

senses.definition: Placed or built with the back against something, especially in architecture or heraldry.
examples: The adossed columns support the outer wall of the church.
An adossed bench ran along the side of the courtyard.
The statues are adossed to the pillars in the old hall.

senses.definition: Something that is placed with its back against another surface, especially in architectural description.
examples: The adossee forms part of the inner wall structure.
Each adossee in the design supports a section of the roof.
The architect explained how the adossee connects to the main pillar.

senses.definition: A rare or dialect word, probably referring to a type of plant or local feature in some regional English varieties.
examples: The old text mentioned an adoulie growing near the stream.
Local names like adoulie can be difficult for outsiders to understand.
In the poem, the adoulie symbolizes resilience.

senses.definition: Down along something or from a higher place to a lower one, used mainly in old or poetic language.
examples: He walked adown the hill in silence.
The river runs adown the valley in the song.
Leaves drifted adown the stream.

senses.definition: Downward or to a lower place, mainly in old or poetic language.
examples: She cast her eyes adown in shame.
The knight rode adown into the dark forest.
Rain came softly falling adown from the gray sky.

senses.definition: A plant of the genus Adoxa, known as moschatel, with small greenish flowers.
examples: The forest floor was dotted with adoxa in early spring.
Adoxa is sometimes called townhall clock for the shape of its flowers.
Botanists recorded several patches of adoxa near the path.

senses.definition: A family of flowering plants that includes the genus Adoxa and some related genera.
examples: Adoxaceae contains shrubs and small trees as well as herbaceous plants.
Some members of Adoxaceae are used in traditional medicine.
The botanist specializes in the Adoxaceae family.

senses.definition: Belonging to or related to the plant family Adoxaceae.
examples: Adoxaceous plants are found in temperate regions.
The garden contains several adoxaceous species.
The adoxaceous characteristics of the shrub were clearly visible.

senses.definition: Plural of adoxy; states of being contrary to accepted belief or opinion, especially in theology or philosophy.
examples: His writings were condemned as dangerous adoxies.
The council gathered to discuss several perceived adoxies.
Throughout history, adoxies have sometimes become new orthodoxies.

senses.definition: Elegant or refined writing about a subject that is unimportant or of little value.
examples: The essay was a clever piece of adoxography praising laziness.
Students were asked to practice adoxography by writing in praise of boredom.
Adoxography was once a popular exercise in rhetorical training.

senses.definition: A belief or opinion that goes against what is generally accepted, especially in religious or philosophical contexts.
examples: His adoxy led to conflict with church authorities.
The book defended what many saw as sheer adoxy.
In that strict community, any adoxy was quickly punished.

senses.definition: Almost asleep or in a light sleep.
examples: He sat adoze in the armchair after lunch.
Passengers were adoze as the train moved slowly through the countryside.
I felt myself growing adoze during the long lecture.

senses.definition: Short for adenosine diphosphate, a chemical compound involved in the transfer of energy in cells.
examples: ATP is converted to ADP when energy is released in the cell.
The cycle between ADP and ATP is essential for muscle movement.
Biology students learn how ADP stores and releases energy.

senses.definition: An abbreviation for Automatic Data Processing, a company that provides payroll and human resources services.
examples: The firm uses ADP for its payroll processing.
ADP offers various human resources solutions to businesses.
Many small companies rely on ADP to manage salaries.

senses.definition: A rare abbreviation, possibly for a specialized organization or technical term, with no widely accepted general meaning.
examples: The report mentioned ADPAO, but did not explain the abbreviation.
Only specialists in the field were familiar with ADPAO.
She searched online to find what ADPAO stood for.

senses.definition: A grammatical word such as a preposition or postposition that shows the relationship between a noun and other parts of a sentence.
examples: In English, most adpositions are prepositions like "in" and "on".
The language also uses postpositions, which are adpositions that follow the noun.
Understanding adposition use is important for mastering sentence structure.

senses.definition: To press something closely against another thing.
examples: Tiny hairs adpress the leaf to the stem.
The technician adpressed the cover firmly into place.
The petals adpress themselves to protect the flower's center.

senses.definition: Lying flat or pressed closely against something, especially in descriptions of plants.
examples: The plant has small adpressed hairs along its stems.
Adpressed leaves can reduce water loss in dry climates.
The scales are adpressed to the body of the fish.

senses.definition: Third person singular of adpress: presses something closely against another thing.
examples: The climbing plant adpresses its stems to the wall.
New growth adpresses itself against the support.
The organism adpresses its body to the rock surface.

senses.definition: Present participle of adpress: pressing something closely against another thing.
examples: Adpressing the leaves to the soil helps them take root.
The botanist described the hairs adpressing the stem.
By adpressing its body to the tree, the insect avoids the wind.

senses.definition: In Roman law, a promise made by a third person to pay someone else’s debt or fulfill another’s obligation.
examples: Adpromission created an additional debtor responsible for the same obligation.
Different types of adpromission existed in classical Roman law.
The lecture on Roman contracts included a section on adpromission.

senses.definition: In Roman law, a person who undertakes adpromission, promising to pay another person’s debt.
examples: The adpromissor became jointly liable with the original debtor.
In the case, the adpromissor was required to fulfill the unpaid obligation.
Roman texts describe the responsibilities of the adpromissor in detail.

senses.definition: Affected by radiation, especially in scientific or technical contexts.
examples: The adrad material had to be handled with care.
Workers avoided the most heavily adrad sections of the plant.
Adrads samples were stored in a special container.

senses.definition: Located away from or not aligned with the radius or central line, especially in anatomy or biology.
examples: The adradial structures are found between the main radial lines.
Adr radial canals branch from the central cavity.
The specimen shows several adradial features.

senses.definition: In a way that is positioned away from or between the main radial lines, especially in anatomy or biology.
examples: The canals extend adradially from the central disc.
Spines are arranged adradially across the surface.
The tissue grows adradially as the organism matures.

senses.definition: A measure or line that lies away from the main radius, especially in geometry or technical descriptions.
examples: The adradius helps define the outer shape of the figure.
Engineers calculated the adradius in their model.
Changes in the adradius affect the overall design.

senses.definition: A deity mentioned in some ancient Near Eastern and biblical sources.
examples: Scholars still debate the origins of Adramelech.
The text refers to Adramelech as a foreign god.
Mythological studies sometimes compare Adramelech to other regional deities.

senses.definition: Variant spelling of Adramelech, a deity known from some ancient and biblical traditions.
examples: In this translation, the name appears as Adrammelech.
Stories about Adrammelech differ between sources.
The cult of Adrammelech is mentioned in the ancient record.

senses.definition: To fear or be afraid of something, in archaic or literary use.
examples: I adread the coming storm.
They adread what the king might decide.
He did not adread the dangers before him.

senses.definition: Past form of adread: feared or was afraid of something, in archaic or literary use.
examples: He adreaded the judgment that awaited him.
They long adreaded the enemy’s return.
She adreaded the news that the messenger would bring.

senses.definition: Present participle of adread: fearing or being afraid of something, in archaic or literary use.
examples: Adreading the worst, they prepared to flee.
He stood adreading the punishment to come.
Adreading failure, she hesitated to speak.

senses.definition: Third person singular of adread: fears or is afraid of something, in archaic or literary use.
examples: He adreads the anger of the gods.
The army adreads what lies beyond the mountains.
She adreads no enemy, however strong.

senses.definition: Lost in dreams or deep thought, as if dreaming.
examples: She sat adream by the window as the rain fell.
He walked along the shore, adream and unaware of time.
Children lay adream on the grass after lunch.

senses.definition: As if in a dream or while dreaming.
examples: She moved adream through the silent house.
The city seemed to float adream in the morning mist.
He answered adream, hardly hearing the question.

senses.definition: Past form of a dialect or literary verb meaning to dream.
examples: Last night I adreamed of distant mountains.
He adreamed of battles and victories.
As a child, she often adreamed of flying.

senses.definition: Variant past form of a literary verb meaning to dream.
examples: All night she adreamt of the old house.
He adreamt of oceans and strange lands.
I have often adreamt of such a place.
